pattern: Row-and-column palindrome symmetry forces every cell into a group of (up to) four mirrored positions that must all end up equal, so the grid decomposes into independent equalization problems plus a tiny leftover mod-4 parity adjustment for any middle row, middle column, and centre cell.
hints: For a grid to be palindromic in BOTH directions at once, cell (i, j) must end up equal to its horizontal mirror, its vertical mirror, and the mirror of both - four cells (fewer at the exact middle) that must all become the same value.

For each group of four mirrored cells, flipping the minority to match the majority is always the cheapest way to make them equal; a leftover middle row or middle column pairs up cells across its own mirror and needs the same treatment two at a time.

After equalizing everything, count how many total 1s you'd have; if it isn't a multiple of 4, use whichever free or cheap adjustment is available - flipping a middle-row/column pair to the other matching value (free, since it was already going to cost one flip either way), toggling the lone centre cell, or sacrificing an already-matched pair - to reach the nearest achievable multiple of 4 at minimum extra cost.

edge_inputs.description: a 3x3 grid needing quadruple equalization plus a centre-cell parity fix
a mismatched middle-row pair with no centre cell available
a two-row single-column grid, only a middle-column style pairing applies
single cell needs one flip to satisfy the divisible-by-four rule
already palindromic in both directions with a ones count divisible by four, needing no flips
an already-valid all-ones 2x2 grid must not be over-flipped
a single wide row exercising row-only symmetry with a centre cell

stress.description: small random grids (at most 16 cells) that exercise the independent exhaustive-search oracle
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    total = max(1, min(size, 16))
    m = rng.randint(1, total)
    n = max(1, total // m)
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [grid]

stress.description: large random rectangular grids near the maximum cell budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    total = max(1, min(size, 200000))
    m = rng.randint(1, min(500, total))
    n = max(1, total // m)
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [grid]
